data.title: Gilead in pact for anti-inflammatory drugs in a deal worth up to $1.7B
data.description:
Gilead Sciences (NASDAQ:GILD) and Danish pharmaceutical company LEO Pharma on Saturday announced an agreement worth up to $1.7B to jointly develop and commercialize LEO’s anti-inflammatory drugs.

As part of the deal, Gilead (NASDAQ:GILD) will buy LEO Pharma’s anti-inflammatory agents, which belong to a drug class known as STAT6 small molecule inhibitors.

The preclinical assets have already demonstrated potential against atopic dermatitis, asthma, COPD, and other inflammatory conditions, the companies said.

While GILD will lead their development as oral treatments, LEO will focus on their topical formulations.

In exchange, LEO is expected to receive up to $1.7B, including $250M as an upfront payment. It is also eligible for royalties ranging from high single digits to mid-teens based on sales of oral products.

Meanwhile, GILD is entitled to royalties ranging from high single digits to mid-teens on sales of topical products.

Foster City, California-based Gilead (GILD) projects the transaction to impact its GAAP and non-GAAP earnings per share by roughly $0.15-$0.17 this year.

data.title: Real estate stocks kick off 2025 on a negative note amid rising longer-term yields
data.description:
Real estate stocks kicked off 2025 on a negative note as rising longer-term Treasury yields continue to worry investors.

Concerns over the national debt and debt ceiling are at play, along with possible inflationary threats like higher tariffs, tax cuts, and mass deportations. Worries over fiscal discipline are also not restricted to the U.S., with borrowing costs in France now above those of Greece, while gilt yields in the U.K. have been pushed to their highest levels since 2008.

Investors are demanding larger premiums in response to the uncertainty.

The S&P 500 slipped 1.94% to end the week at 5,827.04 points. Among the sectors, Real Estate and Technology led the losers.

The Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLRE) closed the holiday-shortened week 4.06% lower at 39.18.

Dow Jones REIT Indx Equity REIT Total Return Index (NASDAQ:REIT:IND) was down 4.22% from last week, while FTSE NAREIT Equity REITS (INDEXFTSE: FNER) fell 3.79%.

"A wider spread and a higher 10-year Treasury yield means higher mortgage rates. So maybe it's time to get re-used to these kinds of mortgage rates that were normal before 2008," said Seeking Alpha author Wolf Richter.

Company News

  • Camden Property Trust (CPT) has been added to the list of companies facing a lawsuit related to rental pricing. The U.S. Department of Justice said the defendants "participated in an unlawful scheme to decrease competition among landlords in apartment pricing, harming millions of American renters."
  • Crown Castle (CCI) CFO Daniel Schlanger will step down from the post, effective March 31.
  • American Tower (AMT) named Eugene "Bud" Noel, currently executive vice president and president of the U.S. Tower division, to the newly created global position of executive vice president and chief operating officer.
  • UBS Securities upgraded Digital Realty Trust (DLR) to Buy from Neutral as analyst John Hodulik believes "stronger renewal spreads, higher yields on new developments and a continuation of the bookings strength realized in '24" should bolster growth over the nearer-term.
  • BXP (BXP) announced the acquisition of 725 12th Street, a 300K sq. ft., 12-storey property in the East End of Washington, DC, for $34M.
  • Simon Property Group (SPG) owned department store chain, JCPenney, and Brookfield Asset Management has merged with SPARC Group to form a new company called Catalyst Brands.
  • The Federal Trade Commission has scheduled a closed-door meeting for next Thursday as FTC Chair Lina Khan's reign nears an end. Potential deals that the FTC may be looking at include CoStar's (CSGP) acquisition of Matterport.

Winners & Losers

This week, BXP (BXP) dragged the Office subsector down among the S&P 500 REIT subsectors. The stock lost ~9% of value. S&P 500 Office REITs closed the week 9.03% lower.

Federal Realty Investment Trust (FRT) was the second major loser among individual stocks.

Welltower (WELL) and Ventas (VTR) were the top performers of the week, but were in the negative territory as well.

Residential REITs was the second major loser among REIT subsectors as mortgage rates continued to inch up.

"In the first full week of the new year, the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage remained elevated at just under 7 percent," Sam Khater, Freddie Mac's chief economist, said.

"The continued strength of the economy has put upward pressure on mortgage rates, and along with high home prices, continues to impact housing affordability. The lack of entry-level supply also remains an issue, especially for those looking to become first-time homeowners," Khater added.

Here is a look at the subsector performance for the week:

data.title: SEC Chair Gensler warns against weakening disclosure rules: report
C-3PO dreams: Tesla aims to sell humanoid robots to consumers in 2026
data.description:
Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) plans to begin selling Optimus robots to consumers in 2026 after producing several thousand this year. The humanoid robot has the potential to transform certain types of household assistance and industrial work.

The humanoid robot, standing at 5'8" tall and weighing about 125 pounds, is designed to perform various tasks in both industrial and domestic settings. Optimus is expected to be able to lift up to 150 pounds and carry 45 pounds while walking at 5 mph. In addition, Optimus is being designed to navigate uneven terrain and maintain balance using onboard sensors.

At CES, Elon Musk hyped the potential of the AI-trained robots. "I think most people, if not everyone, would probably like to have their own personal C-3PO or R2-D2," extolled Musk. "Humanoid robots [can] be the biggest product ever in history, by far," he added. His view is that humanoid robots will eventually outnumber humans by a large margin.

On the business side, Musk thinks humanoid robots will be priced at $20K to $25K each. He sees sales of the Optimus robot hitting 500,000 units by 2027.

On Wall Street, Deutsche Bank analyst Edison Yu estimated Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) might sell 200,00 humanoid robots a year by 2035. He valued the business at $22 a share, or about $70 billion. Bank of America's John Murphy and Wedbush Securities' Dan Ives also think Optimus development is poised to accelerate this year.

On Seeking Alpha, analyst Steven Fiorillo noted that Tesla (TSLA) is in a prime position to utilize its manufacturing expertise to produce Optimus humanoid robots at a scale that competitors are unlikely to match. Meanwhile, analyst Brett Ashcroft Green has a deep dive on how to construct an all-robotics portfolio.

Other companies working on humanoid robot development include Boston Dynamics, Agility Robots, Figure AI, Sanctuary AI, Apptronok, Engineered Arts, OpenAI, Hanson Robotics, UBTech Robotocs, and PAL Robotics. Companies testing industrial and manufacturing use cases for humanoid robots include Amazon (AMZN), BMW (BMWYY), Hyundai (OTCPK:HYMTF), and Mercedes-Benz (OTCPK:MBGAF). Some of those companies are also looking at household applications for a humanoid robot.

data.title: Nvidia at CES: Hit or Flop?
data.description:
Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) made headlines at the just-concluded Consumer Electronics Show (CES) in Las Vegas as its CEO and founder, Jensen Huang, delivered a keynote address and unveiled a slew of new products, kicking off the four-day event.

However, the chipmaker's stock sharply underperformed this week, even though the company showcased some of its most advanced AI innovations during the event, one of the most powerful tech gatherings in the world. While the NASDAQ Composite Index (COMP: IND) fell ~4% last week, Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) dropped ~9% following a massive rally that eclipsed its MAG 7 peers for much of 2024.

Did the company's CES performance disappoint investors or exceed their expectations?

According to Wedbush Securities, Nvidia (NVDA) used the event to extend its "enormous technology lead" in the semiconductor industry and over other tech rivals. "The overriding message from Jensen was a slew of new AI technology is coming out of Nvidia around robotics, autonomous technology, and PCs that will further stretch their enormous technology lead vs. the rest of the semi and Big Tech landscape," analyst Dan Ives wrote.

Meanwhile, Seeking Alpha Investing Group Leader James Foord upgraded the Santa Clara, California-based tech giant after the event, noting that "Nvidia Corporation's CES announcements, including new AI hardware and partnerships, signal a strong future in AI." "Despite the market sell-off, Nvidia's valuation remains attractive with significant growth potential, making it a compelling investment opportunity," Foord added.

According to Seeking Alpha analyst Bob O'Donnell, Nvidia's (NVDA) "most intriguing—but probably least understood"—announcement at CES was Cosmos, an AI-leveraged platform designed to accelerate the development of robotics and autonomous vehicles. Despite a potentially minimal short-term impact, in the longer term, "however, the impact could be profound, as it's expected to dramatically speed up the development of these product categories and improve the accuracy and safety of these applications," O'Donnell argued.

However, as the event came to a close, SA analyst Stuart Allsopp downgraded NVDA on Friday, noting, among other things, the profitability decline in its main customers, its rising account receivables, and Wall Street's peak optimism on the stock. "The failure of the stock to reach new highs recently, combined with recent bearish price action, suggests a potentially significant trend change could be afoot," Allsopp wrote.

data.title: China stops Foxconn from sending Chinese workers to India iPhone factories: Rest of World
data.description:
Foxconn (OTCPK:FXCOF) has stopped new work rotations for its Chinese employees at its Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) iPhone factories in India, and is sending Taiwanese workers instead, Rest of World reported Saturday, citing five people familiar with the manufacturer’s operations in India.

India has spent billions in incentives trying to attract companies like Apple and Samsung Electronics (OTCPK:SSNLF) to set up manufacturing facilities in the country, hoping to capitalize on the 'China-plus-one' strategy as firms look to reduce their reliance on the world's second-largest economy.

Apple (AAPL) has emerged as one of the biggest champions of India's manufacturing efforts, with its annual exports on track to exceed nearly $10 billion for fiscal 2024.

Foxconn Technology (OTCPK:FXCOF) — which is formally known as Hon Hai Precision (OTCPK:HNHAF) (OTCPK:HNHPF) — is the top assembler in India and accounts for half of the nation's iPhone exports. Pegatron and local company Tata Electronics also assemble iPhones in the Asian country.

China and India, two of Asia's largest economies and the world's most populous nations, share a contentious land border.

According to the report, the development is likely to impact iPhone assembly lines in the Foxconn factories in the southern states of Tamil Nadu and Karnataka. Shipments of specialized manufacturing equipment meant for India have also been held up in China.

The report added that the Chinese government is responsible for the suspensions of worker deployments and equipment exports.

To deal with the impact from the suspensions, Foxconn (OTCPK:FXCOF) is planing to move Taiwanese employees, whose travels to India are not impacted, in place of their mainland Chinese colleagues, the report said.

"Maybe China is surprised, as many people were, by how quickly Apple was able to actually move some of its iPhone production to India," Kyle Chan, a postdoctoral research associate with Princeton University who studies industrial policies in China and India, told Rest of World.

Both Apple (AAPL) and Foxconn (OTCPK:FXCOF) did not immediately respond to a Seeking Alpha request for comment.

data.title: Mercury General drops, Riot Platforms climbs in week's financials movers
data.description:
Financial stocks largely retreated in the past week, which featured an extended rally in Treasury yields and hawkish remarks from Federal Reserve officials, denting investor sentiment.

The Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF) slid 1.9% in the five trading sessions ended Jan. 10, worse than the broader S&P 500's 0.7% decrease.

Mercury General (MCY) fell the most of any financial stock (with market cap over $2B), tumbling 26%, after the insurer said it expects losses from the California wildfires to exceed its reinsurance retention level;

Coming at a distant second, Lemonade (LMND), a fellow insurer, gapped down 11.2%;

Jefferies Financial Group (JEF), which during the week posted mixed quarterly results, slumped 9.5%;

RLI Corp. (RLI) dropped 8.5% as a bunch of P&C insurance stocks plunged as they're poised to see billions of dollars of claims from the Los Angeles wildfires; and

India's HDFC Bank (HDB) rounded out the five biggest losers, also falling 8.5%.

For the winners, Riot Platforms (RIOT) took the lead, jumping 14.7%, even as bitcoin (BTC-USD) fell on the week;

Argentine lender BBVA Argentina (BBAR) accelerated 7.6%;

Bitcoin miners Hut 8 (HUT) and CleanSpark (CLSK) gained 6.9% and 6.8%, respectively; and

Swiss bank UBS Group (UBS) ascended 6.7%.

data.title: Enbridge CEO says planning underway to boost capacity under Trump administration: report
Notable analyst calls this week: Tesla, Apple and Boeing stocks among top picks
data.description:
Wall Street on Friday ended lower on Friday, after a hotter-than-expected jobs report dampened hopes for more interest rate cuts. For the week, the S&P (SP500) fell nearly 1%, while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) lost 0.2%. The Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) declined 1.9%

Wall Street had a slew of upgrades and downgrades from analysts. Here are some of the major calls for the week:

Tesla downgraded by BofA on execution risk

Bank of America lowered its rating on Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) to Neutral from Buy, citing high valuation and near-term execution risk.

"While this still implies upside, execution risk is high and TSLA is trading at a level that captures much of our base case LT potential from core autos, robotaxi, Optimus, and energy generation & storage," warned analyst John Murphy, adding that the brokerage is also seeing some risk of new government policies being less favorable for Tesla than expected.

Apple downgraded on high valuation concern

Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) was in focus as the tech giant was downgraded by MoffettNathanson to Sell from Neutral amid concerns about its high valuation.

"The outlook for Apple’s shares, given this challenging backdrop, is, unfortunately, decidedly unattractive, in our view," Moffett added, and put a PT of $188 on the stock.

AT&T upgraded on growth initiatives by RBC, T-Mobile receives rating cut

RBC upgraded AT&T (NYSE:T) to Outperform from Sector Perform, saying that it has confidence in the telecom giant’s growth initiatives, which stand to benefit from subscriber growth anchored by fiber investments going ahead. The brokerage also hiked PT by $4 to $26.

Meanwhile, T-Mobile US (NASDAQ:TMUS) was downgraded to Sector Perform from Outperform. RBC said though execution at the company appears solid, the analysts see less favorable reward/risk based on current valuation. The PT was trimmed by $15 to $240.

Barclays upgrades Boeing on higher plane output

Boeing (NYSE:BA) was upgraded to Overweight from Equal Weight by analysts by Barclays, with analysts saying that the aviation and defense company is poised to gain as its output of planes, especially the best-selling 737 Max, drives higher free cash flow.

Barclays, which raised its PT on the stock to $210 from $190, said Boeing shored up its balance sheet, and it's no longer an overhang, while it believes that new outside leadership can affect meaningful change that's necessary.

Shell upgraded by UBS on hefty free cash flow

Shell (NYSE:SHEL) was upgraded by UBS to Buy from Neutral, even after the company’s disappointing Q4 update. The brokerage noted Shell’s hefty free cash flow and ~14% free cash flow yield that ranks highest among European oil stocks.

UBS analyst Joshua Stone sees an opportunity for $6B of additional cost savings, with the company on track to meet or exceed its 2025 targets.

Analysts bullish on BofA, Citigroup; GS receives downgrade

HSBC and UBS upgraded Bank of America (NYSE:BAC) to Buy, citing the higher-for-longer rate environment as benefiting the money center bank. Truist also initiated coverage of BofA with a Buy rating.

Citigroup (NYSE:C) was also upgraded by Barclays to Overweight from Equalweight, as analyst Jason Goldberg expects accelerated earnings growth, higher investment banking fees, strong operating leverage and greater capital return, among others.

Meanwhile, UBS downgraded Goldman Sachs (NYSE:GS) to Neutral from Buy as the firm's business model relies heavily on Global Banking & Markets, and a "considerable" capital markets recovery is already priced into the stock's valuation.

HSBC downgraded AMD (NASDAQ:AMD) to Reduce from Buy, citing the belief of a "less competitive" offering compared to what Nvidia has. Analyst Frank Lee, who also reduced PT to $110 from $200, said AMD is unlikely to "penetrate" the AI GPU market as much as previously thought.

Stifel said Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) remains its "best idea" for artificial intelligence and accelerated computing in 2025. The brokerage thinks that the company will likely maintain its strong leadership position in the AI infrastructure market as its Blackwell architecture ramp begins in earnest in 2025.

Palo Alto Networks (NASDAQ:PANW) was downgraded by Deutsche Bank and BTIG to Hold from Buy and cut its price target to $190 from $207 amid an expected slowdown in security spending this year.

data.title: Wall Street's tough week pushes S&P 500 into negative territory for the new year
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SP500) on Friday slipped 1.94% for the holiday-shortened week to end at 5,827.04 points, posting losses in two out of four sessions. Its accompanying SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (NYSEARCA:SPY) also fell 1.94% for the week.

It was a sobering week for Wall Street, which left the benchmark index negative for the new year. The S&P (SP500) is now -4.48% from its record intraday high of 6,099.97 points. In the six trading days of 2025 so far, the gauge has ended in the red three times, including two drops of more than 1%.

A flood of economic data on the labor market and inflation this week indicated high resilience in the former and concerning signals regarding the latter. In response, market participants rushed to recalibrate their Federal Reserve interest rate cut bets.

Several central bank policymakers also hinted that monetary policy easing might be put on hold. Governor Michelle Bowman event went on to say that she "could have supported taking no action at the December (monetary policy committee) meeting," citing a "lack of continued progress on lowering inflation and the ongoing strength in economic activity and in the labor market."

According to the CME FedWatch tool, traders are now pricing in only two quarter-point interest rate cuts in 2025. Major brokerages also dialed back their expectations, with JPMorgan and Goldman Sachs also predicting just two rate cuts this year. Bank of America one-upped them and said it sees no more rate cuts at all, adding that there appeared to be greater risks toward policymakers now raising rates.

The first jolt this week came on Tuesday, ironically in the form of the November Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS). Job openings surged, while the quits rate edged down. Following a market holiday on Thursday for a national day of mourning for former U.S. President Jimmy Carter, investors returned on Friday with their sights squarely set on the December nonfarm payrolls report.

The data showed a whopping 256K jobs added to the U.S. economy, easily outstripping the consensus of +157K. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate ticked down to 4.1%, coming in lower than the expected figure of 4.2%.

Both indicators suggest that labor market has once again become highly resilient after showing considerable cooling in the middle of last year.

Meanwhile, in terms of inflation, prices paid by services organizations for materials and services increased to a nearly two-year high in December. Meanwhile, a gauge of long-run inflation expectations among consumers soared to its highest level since 2008.

Wall Street's focus now shifts to the earnings season, which will kick off in earnest in the second half of January. The political landscape is also very much on investors' minds, with incoming U.S. President Donald Trump's inauguration fast approaching.

"The weight of the evidence in our work suggests we are seeing a reset of market prices and sentiment, which had become stretched on a short-term basis, though it is still within the confines of an ongoing bull market," Keith Lerner, co-chief investment officer at Truist, said on Friday.

"When expectations are high, a little bad news can go a long way. And the proximate cause of the recent market setback has been the sharp move higher in interest rates. Our long-held view remains that we would prefer a stronger economy with fewer rate cuts than a weaker economy that requires more aggressive rate cuts," Lerner added.

Turning to the weekly performance of the S&P 500 (SP500) sectors, eight of the 11 ended in negative territory. Real Estate and Technology led the losers. Energy, Health Care and Materials were the three winners. See below a breakdown of the performance of the sectors as well as their accompanying SPDR Select Sector ETFs from January 3 close to January 10 close:

#1: Energy +0.90%, and the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLE) +1.05%.

#2: Health Care +0.52%, and the Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLV) +0.49%.

#3: Materials +0.14%, and the Materials Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLB) +0.13%.

#4: Communication Services -0.70%, and the Communication Services Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLC) -2.11%.

#5: Industrials -1.01%, and the Industrial Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLI) -1.05%.

#6: Consumer Staples -1.92%, and the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLP) -2.54%.

#7: Utilities -1.96%, and the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLU) -1.95%.

#8: Consumer Discretionary -2.34%, and the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLY) -2.24%.

#9: Financials -2.71%, and the Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLF) -2.61%.

#10: Information Technology -3.10%, and the Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLK) -2.96%.

#11: Real Estate -4.10%, and the Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLRE) -4.06%.

For investors looking to track the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500), here are some exchange-traded funds of interest: (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), and (SPXU).

For investors looking into the future of what's happening, take a look at the Seeking Alpha Catalyst Watch to see next week's breakdown of actionable events that stand out.
